description: The visible image is an unrelated software interface button printed with the text "Check for updates", which does not match the target figure for the low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel study. No valid information related to the steel's hot deformation behavior, dynamic recrystallization characteristics, microstructure evolution, or mechanical performance can be extracted from this displayed image, indicating the target research figure failed to load correctly. All relevant research contents mentioned in the text extraction summary cannot be verified by the currently visible image.

This is an optical micrograph showing the initial microstructure of Fe-Mn-Al-C steel, with a visible scale bar of 100 μm. The investigated low-density high-strength steel has a nominal chemical composition (mass fraction) of 1.46% C, 25.14% Mn, 10% Al, 0.053% Nb, and balance Fe. The as-received microstructure consists of equiaxed austenite grains with clear etched grain boundaries, and distinct annealing twin structures are observed inside most grains. This initial microstructure acts as the baseline reference for subsequent analysis of microstructure evolution and dynamic recrystallization behavior of the steel during isothermal hot compression tests performed at 900-1150 °C with strain rates ranging from 0.01 s⁻¹ to 10 s⁻¹, supporting the development of dynamic recrystallization models and grain size prediction for this steel grade.

This figure presents the temperature-time process flow of isothermal constant strain rate compression experiments for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density high-strength steel, with the vertical axis representing temperature (°C) and the horizontal axis representing time (s). The experimental procedure is as follows: 1) Heat the sample to 1200 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/s, hold for 120 s for homogenization; 2) Cool to the preset deformation temperature (range: 900-1150 °C) at a cooling rate of 5 °C/s, hold for 15 s to ensure uniform internal temperature of the sample; 3) Perform isothermal compression at strain rates of 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10 s⁻¹, with a maximum compression reduction of 60% (corresponding to maximum true strain 0.92); 4) Quench the sample immediately after compression to retain the high-temperature deformed microstructure. This figure provides a clear standardized processing protocol for subsequent studies on hot deformation behavior, dynamic recrystallization and microstructure evolution of the tested steel.

This figure presents hot deformation mechanical performance of low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel. Subplot (a) shows true stress-true strain curves at a fixed strain rate of 0.01 s⁻¹ under deformation temperatures from 900 ℃ to 1150 ℃: true stress decreases with increasing temperature, all curves rise to a peak stress then drop to a steady state, indicating dynamic recrystallization occurs during deformation. Subplot (b) shows true stress-true strain curves at fixed 1100 ℃ under strain rates from 0.01 s⁻¹ to 10 s⁻¹: true stress increases with rising strain rate. Subplots (c) and (d) are 3D colored plots displaying peak stress as a function of temperature and strain rate in 0.01-0.1 s⁻¹ and 1-10 s⁻¹ ranges respectively, peak stress monotonically decreases with increasing temperature and decreasing strain rate, ranging from ~53 MPa (1150 ℃, 0.01 s⁻¹) to ~486 MPa (900 ℃, 10 s⁻¹). The results confirm the steel has negative temperature sensitivity and positive strain rate sensitivity for flow stress, providing fundamental data for its hot working process design.

This group of plots presents linear fitting results for the hot deformation constitutive relationship of low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel. Subplot (a) shows the fitting of ln(ε̇) versus ln[sinh(ασₚ)] at deformation temperatures of 900, 950, 1000, 1050, 1100, and 1150 ℃, with an average slope k₁=5.135. Subplot (b) shows the fitting of ln[sinh(ασₚ)] versus 1000T⁻¹ at strain rates of 0.01, 0.1, 1, and 10 s⁻¹, with an average slope k₂=15.690. The imperfect parallelism of fitted lines in both subplots indicates deformation conditions have a significant effect on dynamic recrystallization (DRX) activation energy. Based on the two slopes, the average DRX activation energy of the studied steel is calculated as 669.844 kJ·mol⁻¹, which supports the establishment of the Zener-Hollomon parameter-based hot deformation constitutive model and hot processing optimization for this steel.

This figure shows the linear fitting analysis of the relationship between ln(σ/σ_p) and ln(ε/ε_p)+1−ε/ε_p for low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel subjected to isothermal hot compression at 1050 °C and strain rate of 0.01 s⁻¹. Grey cross symbols denote discrete experimental data points obtained from hot deformation tests, while the red line represents the linear fit of these data points. The fitted material constant C is 0.1461, with a correlation coefficient R of 0.9728, confirming a strong linear correlation between the two parameters. This figure validates the method that the material constant C can be derived from the slope of this linear fit, and the method can be applied to acquire C values under other hot deformation temperatures and strain rates for the studied Fe-Mn-Al-C steel to support dynamic recrystallization model construction.

This set of three subfigures demonstrates the relationship between characteristic values of rheological stress curves and the natural logarithm of Zener-Hollomon parameter (lnZ) for low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel. Subfigure (a) presents critical stress σ_c (star markers, fitting correlation coefficient R=0.947) and critical strain ε_c (hollow circle markers, R=0.911) as functions of lnZ. Subfigure (b) shows peak stress σ_p (star markers, R=0.957) and peak strain ε_p (hollow circle markers, R=0.911) against lnZ. Subfigure (c) exhibits steady-state stress σ_s (star markers, R=0.956) and steady-state strain ε_s (hollow circle markers, R=0.922) versus lnZ. All studied characteristic stresses and strains increase with rising lnZ: stresses are fitted with non-linear curves, while strains are fitted with linear curves, all R values above 0.91 indicate good fitting quality. This figure supports the establishment of quantitative models between dynamic recrystallization characteristic parameters and Z parameter, providing data support for hot working process optimization of the studied steel.

This figure is the dynamic recrystallization (DRX) state diagram for low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel under hot deformation. The x-axis is true strain ε ranging from 0.0 to 0.9, and the y-axis is the natural logarithm of the Zener-Hollomon parameter lnZ ranging from 50 to 70. Hollow circles represent critical DRX strain εc, while red stars represent steady-state DRX strain εs. The diagram is divided into three regions: region A (work hardening zone), region B (partial DRX zone), and region C (complete DRX zone). Both εc and εs exhibit positive linear correlation with lnZ. As lnZ, which increases with rising strain rate or decreasing deformation temperature, increases, the required strain for DRX initiation and completion rises, indicating DRX becomes more difficult at higher Z conditions. This diagram supports the establishment of hot processing windows for the studied steel to obtain desired DRX microstructures.

This schematic illustrates the different deformation zones on the cross-section parallel to the loading direction of a low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel sample after hot compression, with a vertical downward loading force P applied. Three distinct zones are marked: Zone 1 is the difficult deformation zone near the upper and lower end faces, restricted by friction between the sample and pressure head; Zone 2 is the small deformation zone distributed near the two rounded end edges; Zone 3 is the large deformation zone in the sample central area with the highest deformation degree. Non-uniform stress distribution induced by interfacial friction during hot compression leads to the formation of these zones. This schematic provides the basis for subsequent microstructure observation of different zones to analyze dynamic recrystallization behavior and microstructure evolution of the tested steel, supporting the conclusion that dynamic recrystallization degree and grain size vary with deformation magnitude in different zones.

This figure presents 6 groups of optical metallographic micrographs showing the microstructure of low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel after hot isothermal compression under different deformation temperatures (900–1150 °C) and strain rates (0.01–10 s⁻¹). Each group includes three micrographs corresponding to three deformation zones of the compressed sample: Zone 1 (difficult deformation zone), Zone 2 (small deformation zone), Zone 3 (large deformation zone), with a 100 μm scale bar provided for each micrograph. Observations show dynamic recrystallization (DRX) degree follows the order large deformation zone > small deformation zone > difficult deformation zone, indicating DRX is promoted by higher strain. DRX grain size increases with elevated temperature or reduced strain rate: under low temperature (900–950 °C) and high strain rate (1–10 s⁻¹), DRX is incomplete with residual coarse original grains and poor homogeneity, while high temperature/low strain rate produces coarser fully recrystallized grains. This figure validates DRX evolution dependence on deformation parameters and local strain state, supporting hot processing optimization for the steel.

This plot presents the average dynamic recrystallization grain size of low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel after 60% hot compression, as a function of temperature (ranging from 900 °C to 1150 °C) under four different strain rates: 0.01 s⁻¹ (black line with square markers), 0.1 s⁻¹ (red line with circle markers), 1 s⁻¹ (blue line with triangle markers), and 10 s⁻¹ (purple line with star markers). The y-axis represents grain size in μm, while the x-axis is deformation temperature in °C. For all strain rates, the DRX grain size monotonically increases with rising temperature. At the same temperature, lower strain rate leads to larger grain size: at 1150 °C and 0.01 s⁻¹, grain size exceeds 70 μm, while it is only around 5 μm at 900 °C and 10 s⁻¹. The figure confirms that DRX grain size of the studied steel is positively correlated with deformation temperature and negatively correlated with strain rate, supporting the establishment of the grain size prediction model for hot processing optimization of this steel.

This figure consists of three subplots to validate the dynamic recrystallization grain size prediction model for low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel processed under isothermal hot compression at 900-1150 °C with strain rates of 0.01, 0.1, 1, and 10 s⁻¹. Subplot (a) shows experimental grain size as a function of deformation temperature: grain size increases with rising temperature and decreasing strain rate, ranging from ~4 μm at 900 °C/10 s⁻¹ to ~75 μm at 1150 °C/0.01 s⁻¹. Subplot (b) presents the correlation between predicted and experimental grain size, with a linear fit correlation coefficient R=0.996, mean relative error (MRE) of 4.53%, and all data points lying within ±10% deviation bands. Subplot (c) illustrates the absolute error between predicted and experimental values, most of which are below 3 μm. The results confirm the established model has high prediction accuracy, providing reliable theoretical support for dynamic recrystallized grain size prediction of the studied steel after hot forging.
